sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 65-year-old male, presents with a nonhealing foot ulcer on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, which has been present for approximately two months.
The ulcer is now red, draining, and has become more painful with throbbing sensations.
The patient reports that the ulcer started after walking barefoot on the beach.
The patient's primary care physician (PCP) started him on antibiotics, but he has not yet picked them up from the pharmacy.
The patient's diabetes is not well-controlled, with an A1c of approximately 10%.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes, but the details of his management and control are unclear.
Review of systems: The patient reports occasional throbbing pain in the foot.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are within normal limits.
Physical exam: A 1x2 inch circular wound is present on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the fifth MTP joint.
The wound is red, draining, and has edema around it.
There is fluid present, but no necrosis, odor, or bony exposure.
Test Results: An x-ray of the right foot shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with a suspected infection, likely caused by poor blood supply due to uncontrolled diabetes.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; Suspected infection; Uncontrolled diabetes; Poor blood supply to the foot.
Plan:
I prescribed clindamycin 400mg twice daily for 7 days to treat the suspected infection.
I will perform a debridement to remove dead tissue and order an ABI (ankle-brachial index) to assess blood supply to the foot.
I will also provide a surgical shoe to reduce pressure on the foot and promote healing.
The patient will be seen back in two weeks for a follow-up examination and possible additional debridement.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen in two weeks for a follow-up examination.
